Let's discover Docusaurus in less than 5 minutes.
An online mathematical encyclopedia devoted to Deriving and exploring mathematics beyond the classroom for high school students. All Things Are Derivable

Our mission is to make mathematical reasoning transparent, tangible, and telling through Derivation while offering additional pathways for inquisitive and motivated students to explore mathematics with greter rigor and depth.
This project aims to demystify mathematical Deriving and to foster deeper curiosity through clear and careful reasoning.

Throughout this project, Derivation is treated as a proper noun, referring to the process of obtain a result through meticulous, step-by-step calculation. As the main theme of Art of Derivation, all words related to Derivation (such as Derive, Deriving) are highlighted in the main theme color. Some closely related terms, though not strictly equivalent, including Proof, Justification, and Logical Argument, all of which are integral to the idea of Derivation presented in this project. My understanding of Derivation was mainly developed from my AP Physics C: Mechanics course, where the Free Response Questions frequently require student to Derive expressions from fundamental definitions or equations in the provided equations sheet.
This project is built on Docusaurus and deployed on GitHub Pages. Below are detailed format information.

All documents are stored as Markdown files and use either the .md or .mdx extension. All document file names are capitalized and connected using hyphens (-). All documents are stored under the docs directory, organized by sidebars.js. All referenced images (such as .jpg, .png, .svg) are stored in the static/img directory.
All documents start with the front matter to ensure the consistent format and navigation. More additional details, refer to Markdown Features | Docusaurus.
All mathematical expressions are rendered using KaTeX with remark-math and follow standard LaTeX\LaTeXLATE​X syntax.

All Derivation processes are placed in collapsible blocks, implemented using <details>+<summary>. All Derivation processes are organized using the align* enviornment. All Derivation processes are completed with \blacksquare (■\blacksquare■), indicating Q.E.D. (quod erat demonstrandum), meaning "that which was to be demonstrated".

Note The Gray Admonition provides extra and unnecessary notes and information. Definition The Green Admonition provides definition about the given topic. Property, Identity, Solution... The Blue Admonition provides Properties, Identities, Solutions, and so on that is NOT designed to show the collapsable Derivation process. Derivation The collapsable Admonition provides detailed process for Derivation Warning The Orange Admonition provides information that is important to know.

The theme color of this project is Indigo (#4b0082) in light mode and Magenta (#ff61ff) in dark mode. The theme color is exclusively reserved for Derivation and its related concepts, rather than decorative styling.
The idea of the favicon came from ∵\because∵ ($\because$) and ∴\therefore∴ ($\therefore$).

All documents are organized in a logical sequence; following the given order is strongly recommended. Before viewing the full derivation through the collapsable bar, attempt to work through each step independently using pen and paper. Detailed solutions are provided in the collapsible sections for verification and deeper understanding. Maintaining a dedicated notebook for your own derivations is highly encouraged.

Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality Real Number Case The following version of the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality is for real numbers only; more general forms exist in complex and vector settings. Given two finite number sequence in the real number {x1,x2,⋯ ,xn}\{x_1,x_2,\cdots,x_n\}{x1​,x2​,⋯,xn​} and {y1,y2,⋯ ,yn}\{y_1,y_2,\cdots,y_n\}{y1​,y2​,⋯,yn​}, the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality states: (∑i=1nxiyi)2≤(∑i=1nxi2)(∑i=1nyi2)\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_iy_i \right)^2\leq \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i^2 \right)\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}y_i^2 \right)(i=1∑n​xi​yi​)2≤(i=1∑n​xi2​)(i=1∑n​yi2​) in which (x1y1+x2y2+⋯+xnyn)2≤(x12+x22+⋯+xn2)(y12+y22+⋯+yn2)(x_1y_1+x_2y_2+\cdots +x_n y_n)^2\leq (x_1^2+x_2^2+\cdots+x_n^2)(y_1^2+y_2^2+\cdots+y_n^2)(x1​y1​+x2​y2​+⋯+xn​yn​)2≤(x12​+x22​+⋯+xn2​)(y12​+y22​+⋯+yn2​) This inequality equal when there exists a real number λ\lambdaλ such that xi=λbix_i=\lambda b_ixi​=λbi​

This page lists several fundamental integrals and their basic properties. Their Derivations are mostly straightforward, as they follow directly from reversing those common derivatives.

Non-negotiable The following integrals are essential formulas that should be memorized and known by heart, as they will be used frequently throughout the subsequent Derivations sections. Below are 147 advanced integral formulas listed in the appendix of the Higher Mathematics (Tongji University).Each formula is Derived step by step. It may seem futile and impractival to memorize all the formulas, but it is a great opportunity to consolidate integration techniques and appreciate heuristics behind them.

In my AP Physics C Mechanics class, many students were struggling to Derive the terminal velocity of a free-falling object, and the primary reason was that they can't solve the Integral Formula #21. My teacher back then found a really sophisticated way through hyperbolic trigonometry substitution, and everyone was stunned by the complexity, but I came up with a simple algebraic transformation. Then, I decided to Derive those formulas as a way to unveil those "secrets" beyond the general textbook. 📄️ Integral with ax+b Formula 1-9 📄️ Integral with √(ax+b) Formula 10-18 📄️ Integral with x²±a² Formula 19-21 📄️ Integral with ax²+b Formula 22-28 📄️ Integral with ax²+bx+c Formula 28-30 📄️ Integral with √(x²+a²) Formula 31-44 📄️ Integral with √(x²-a²) Formula 45-58 📄️ Integral with √(a²-x²) Formula 59-72 📄️ Integral with √(±ax²+bx+c) Formula 73-78 📄️ Integral with √[±(x-a)/(x-b)] Formula 79-80 📄️ Integral with √[(x-a)(b-x)] Formula 81-82 📄️ Integral with Trig Functions Formula 83-112 📄️ Integral with Inverse Trig Functions Formula 113-121 📄️ Integral with Exponential Functions Formula 122-131 📄️ Integral with Logarithmic Functions Formula 132-136 📄️ Integral with Hyperbolic Functions Formula 137-141 📄️ Definite Integrals Formula 142-147

Substitution is the most common integral technique in elementary calculus. Recall what we mentioned in common integrals that we use uuu and du\mathrm{d}udu instead of xxx and dx\mathrm{d}xdx, and this is because uuu may represent an entire function, not just a single variable. We will tackle with this more specifically in this section.

We will start with this integral question: ∫3x2sin⁡x3 dx= ?\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\ ?∫3x2sinx3 dx= ? Note that there is an product, which is beyond all common integrals, so we need to simplify to an easier form. An example is below: Solution Let: u=x3u=x^3u=x3 dudx=3x2du=3x2 dx\begin{align*} \frac{\mathrm{d}u}{\mathrm{d}x}&=3x^2\\ \color{red}{\mathrm{d}u}&=\color{red}{3x^2\ \mathrm{d}x}\\ \end{align*}dxdu​du​=3x2=3x2 dx​ Plug in back to the question: ∫3x2sin⁡x3 dx=∫sin⁡u du=∫sin⁡u du=−cos⁡u+C=−cos⁡x3+C\begin{align*} \int{\color{red}{3x^2}}\sin{x^3}\ \color{red}{\mathrm{d}x}&=\int{\sin{u}}\ \color{red}{\mathrm{d}u}\\ &=\int{\sin{u}}\ \mathrm{d}u\\ &=-\cos{u}+C\\ &=-\cos{x^3}+C \end{align*}∫3x2sinx3 dx​=∫sinu du=∫sinu du=−cosu+C=−cosx3+C​ Hence: ∫3x2sin⁡x3 dx=−cos⁡x3+C■\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=-\cos{x^3}+C \tag*{$\blacksquare$}∫3x2sinx3 dx=−cosx3+C■ By replacing the variable xxx into a function of uuu, we can simplify the overall question to a common integral. As you can see, this is a method we inversely use the Chain Rule, and generally, it is called: The substitution rule Given a differenciable function u=g(x)u=g(x)u=g(x), and fff is continuous on its interval, then: ∫f(g(x))g′(x) dx=∫f(u) du\int{f\left(g\left(x\right)\right)g'(x)}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int{f\left(u\right)}\ \mathrm{d}u∫f(g(x))g′(x) dx=∫f(u) du Definite Integral In the case of Definite Integral, the bound should also changes, as: ∫abf(g(x))g′(x) dx=∫g(a)g(b)f(u) du\int_{a}^{b}{f\left(g\left(x\right)\right)g'(x)}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int_{g(a)}^{g(b)}{f\left(u\right)}\ \mathrm{d}u∫ab​f(g(x))g′(x) dx=∫g(a)g(b)​f(u) du

Direct Substitution is a method created by the author himself. It simplify the process of substitution without write out uuu and du\mathrm{d}udu. I will show this with an example: ∫3x2sin⁡x3 dx= ?\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\ ?∫3x2sinx3 dx= ? This time, let's try what I called Direct Substituton: Direct Substitution Approach After analyze, we want to substitute x3x^3x3. All we need to do is substitude dx\mathrm{d}xdx to a fraction where the top is dx3\mathrm{d}x^3dx3 and the bottom is the derivative of x3x^3x3 based on xxx, as: ∫3x2sin⁡x3 dx=∫3x2sin⁡x3 d(x3)3x2=∫sin⁡x3 d(x3)\int{3x^2\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int{\cancel{3x^2}\sin{x^3}}\ \frac{\mathrm{d}(x^3)}{\cancel{3x^2}}=\int{\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}(x^3)∫3x2sinx3 dx=∫3x2sinx3 3x2d(x3)​=∫sinx3 d(x3) And all we need is to consider x3x^3x3 as uuu and integrate normally: ⟹ ∫sin⁡x3 d(x3)=−cos⁡x3+C\implies \int{\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}(x^3)=-\cos{x^3}+C⟹∫sinx3 d(x3)=−cosx3+C More importantly, for definite integral, it doesn't need to change bounds, as: ∫ab3x2sin⁡x3 dx=∫absin⁡x3 d(x3)=−cos⁡x3∣ab=−cos⁡b3+cos⁡a3\int_{a}^{b}3x^2\sin{x^3}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int_{a}^{b}{\sin{x^3}}\ \mathrm{d}(x^3)=-\cos{x^3}\big|_{a}^{b}=-\cos{b^3}+\cos{a^3}∫ab​3x2sinx3 dx=∫ab​sinx3 d(x3)=−cosx3​ab​=−cosb3+cosa3 Direct Substitution In general, if we want to substitude ax+bax+bax+b or term that can cancel the other one, we may use direct substitution ∫f(x)g(x)⏟Substitude dx=∫f(x)g(x) d(g(x))g′(x)\int{{f(x)}\underbrace{g(x)}_{\text{Substitude}}}\ \mathrm{d}x=\int{f(x)g(x)}\ \frac{\mathrm{d}(g(x))}{g'(x)} ∫f(x)Substitudeg(x)​​ dx=∫f(x)g(x) g′(x)d(g(x))​

If Cevians ADADAD, BEBEBE, CFCFCF all pass through pint OOO, then according to Italian mathematician Giovanni Ceva, we have Ceva's Theorem says: AD‾DB‾⋅BE‾EC‾⋅CF‾FA‾=1 \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BE}}{\overline{EC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CF}}{\overline{FA}}=1DBAD​⋅ECBE​⋅FACF​=1 Where AD‾\overline{AD}AD represents the length of segment ADADAD Derivation Notice that the segment AD{AD}AD and segment DB{DB}DB share a same base ABABAB. ⟹ AD‾DB‾=[△AOD][△BOD]=[△ACD][△BCD]∴AD‾DB‾=[△ACD]−[△AOD][△BCD]−[△BOD]=[△ACO][△BCO] \begin{align*} \implies \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}&=\frac{[\triangle AOD]}{[\triangle BOD]}=\frac{[\triangle ACD]}{[\triangle BCD]}\\ \therefore \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}&=\frac{[\triangle ACD]-[\triangle AOD]}{[\triangle BCD]-[\triangle BOD]}= \frac{[\triangle ACO]}{[\triangle BCO]} \end{align*}⟹DBAD​∴DBAD​​=[△BOD][△AOD]​=[△BCD][△ACD]​=[△BCD]−[△BOD][△ACD]−[△AOD]​=[△BCO][△ACO]​​ Similarly, we may also et: BE‾EC‾=[△BAO][△CAO], CF‾FA‾=[△CBO][△ABO] \frac{\overline{BE}}{\overline{EC}}=\frac{[\triangle BAO]}{[\triangle CAO]}, \ \frac{\overline{CF}}{\overline{FA}}=\frac{[\triangle CBO]}{[\triangle ABO]}ECBE​=[△CAO][△BAO]​, FACF​=[△ABO][△CBO]​ Hence: AD‾DB‾⋅BE‾EC‾⋅CF‾FA‾=[△ACO][△BCO]⋅[△ABO][△ACO]⋅[△BCO][△ABO]=1■ \frac{\overline{AD}}{\overline{DB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BE}}{\overline{EC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CF}}{\overline{FA}}= \frac{[\triangle ACO]}{[\triangle BCO]}\cdot \frac{[\triangle ABO]}{[\triangle ACO]}\cdot \frac{[\triangle BCO]}{[\triangle ABO]}=1 \tag*{$\blacksquare$}DBAD​⋅ECBE​⋅FACF​=[△BCO][△ACO]​⋅[△ACO][△ABO]​⋅[△ABO][△BCO]​=1■ Note: [△AOD][\triangle AOD][△AOD] represents the area of △AOD\triangle AOD△AOD

Heron's Theorem Given a △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC with the side length aaa, bbb, and ccc, then, the area is: S△ABC=p(p−a)(p−b)(p−c) S_{\triangle{ABC}}=\sqrt{p(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)}S△ABC​=p(p−a)(p−b)(p−c)​ where p=a+b+c2p=\frac{a+b+c}{2}p=2a+b+c​, as half of the semiperimeter of △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC Qin Jiushao's formula Alternatively, here is an equivalent form of Heron's formula S△ABC=14[a2c2−(a2−b2+c22)2] S_{\triangle{ABC}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\left[a^2c^2-\left(\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2}\right)^2 \right]}S△ABC​=41​[a2c2−(2a2−b2+c2​)2]​ Derivation Let BH⊥ACBH \perp ACBH⊥AC; HHH is a point on ACACAC. From the Pythagorean Theorem, we get: a2−x2=b2−(c−x)2a2−x2=b2−c2+2cx−x22cx=a2−b2+c2x=a2−b2+c22c\begin{align*} a^2-x^2&=b^2-(c-x)^2\\ a^2-x^2&=b^2-c^2+2cx-x^2\\ 2cx&=a^2-b^2+c^2\\ x&=\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2c} \end{align*}a2−x2a2−x22cxx​=b2−(c−x)2=b2−c2+2cx−x2=a2−b2+c2=2ca2−b2+c2​​ S△ABC=12AC⋅BH=12a⋅c2−(a2−b2+c22c)2=14[a2c2−(a2−b2+c22)2](Qin Jiushao’s formula)=116[4a2c2−(a2−b2+c2)2]=(2ac)2−(a2−b2+c2)216=(2ac+a2−b2+c2)(2ac−a2+b2−c2)16=[(a+c)2−b2][b2−(a−c)2]16=(a+c+b)(a+c−b)(b+a−c)(b−a+c)16=a+b+c2−a+b+c2a−b+c2a+b−c2=a+b+c2(a+b+c2−a)(a+b+c2−b)(a+b+c2−c) \begin{align*} S_{\triangle{ABC}}&=\frac{1}{2}AC\cdot BH \\ &=\frac{1}{2}a\cdot \sqrt{c^2-\left(\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2c}\right)^2}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\left[a^2c^2-\left(\frac{a^2-b^2+c^2}{2}\right)^2 \right]} &&\text{(Qin Jiushao's formula)}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{1}{16}[4a^2c^2-(a^2-b^2+c^2)^2]}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{(2ac)^2-(a^2-b^2+c^2)^2}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{(2ac+a^2-b^2+c^2)(2ac-a^2+b^2-c^2)}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{[(a+c)^2-b^2][b^2-(a-c)^2]}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{(a+c+b)(a+c-b)(b+a-c)(b-a+c)}{16}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{a+b+c}{2}\frac{-a+b+c}{2}\frac{a-b+c}{2}\frac{a+b-c}{2}}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{a+b+c}{2}\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-a\right)\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-b\right)\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-c\right)}\\ \end{align*}S△ABC​​=21​AC⋅BH=21​a⋅c2−(2ca2−b2+c2​)2​=41​[a2c2−(2a2−b2+c2​)2]​=161​[4a2c2−(a2−b2+c2)2]​=16(2ac)2−(a2−b2+c2)2​​=16(2ac+a2−b2+c2)(2ac−a2+b2−c2)​​=16[(a+c)2−b2][b2−(a−c)2]​​=16(a+c+b)(a+c−b)(b+a−c)(b−a+c)​​=2a+b+c​2−a+b+c​2a−b+c​2a+b−c​​=2a+b+c​(2a+b+c​−a)(2a+b+c​−b)(2a+b+c​−c)​​(Qin Jiushao’s formula) Now, let p=a+b+c2p=\frac{a+b+c}{2}p=2a+b+c​, then: S△ABC=p(p−a)(p−b)(p−c)■ S_{\triangle{ABC}}=\sqrt{p(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}S△ABC​=p(p−a)(p−b)(p−c)​■

Menelaus's Theorem Menelaus's theorem is named for Greek Mathematician Menelaus of Alexandria that said: given a line and a △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC, if the line cross through the sides of △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC ABABAB, ACACAC, and BCBCBC or their extension at point LLL, MMM, and NNN as shown in graph, then: AM‾MB‾⋅BL‾LC‾⋅CN‾NA‾=1 \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}=1MBAM​⋅LCBL​⋅NACN​=1 where AM‾\overline{AM}AM represents the length of segment AMAMAM Derivation Derivation 1: Areas Connect ALALAL, as shown on the left. Since there are some triangles share the same base and altitude: AM‾MB‾=[△ALM][△BLM],BL‾LC‾=[△BLM][△CLM],CN‾NA‾=[△CLM][△ALM] \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}=\frac{[\triangle ALM]}{[\triangle BLM]}, \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}}=\frac{[\triangle BLM]}{[\triangle CLM]}, \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}=\frac{[\triangle CLM]}{[\triangle ALM]}MBAM​=[△BLM][△ALM]​,LCBL​=[△CLM][△BLM]​,NACN​=[△ALM][△CLM]​ Hence: AM‾MB‾⋅BL‾LC‾⋅CN‾NA‾=[△ALM][△BLM]⋅[△BLM][△CLM]⋅[△CLM][△ALM]=1■ \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}=\frac{[\triangle ALM]}{[\triangle BLM]}\cdot \frac{[\triangle BLM]}{[\triangle CLM]} \cdot \frac{[\triangle CLM]}{[\triangle ALM]} =1 \tag*{$\blacksquare$}MBAM​⋅LCBL​⋅NACN​=[△BLM][△ALM]​⋅[△CLM][△BLM]​⋅[△ALM][△CLM]​=1■ Note: [△AOD][\triangle AOD][△AOD] represents the area of △AOD\triangle AOD△AOD Derivation 2: Trigonometry Let ∠AMN=∠BML=α\angle AMN=\angle BML=\alpha∠AMN=∠BML=α, ∠ANM=β\angle ANM=\beta∠ANM=β, ∠BLM=γ\angle BLM=\gamma∠BLM=γ. According to the Law of Sines, in △AMN\triangle AMN△AMN: NA‾sin⁡α=AM‾sin⁡β ⟹ AM‾NA‾=sin⁡βsin⁡α\frac{\overline{NA}}{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{{\overline{AM}}}{\sin{\beta}} \implies \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{NA}}= \frac{\sin{\beta}}{\sin{\alpha}}sinαNA​=sinβAM​⟹NAAM​=sinαsinβ​ Similarly, in △BLM\triangle{BLM}△BLM and △CLM\triangle{CLM}△CLM, we get: BL‾MB‾=sin⁡αsin⁡γ, CN‾LC‾=sin⁡γsin⁡(180°−β)=sin⁡γsin⁡β\frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{MB}}= \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{\sin{\gamma}},\ \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{LC}}= \frac{\sin{\gamma}}{\sin{(180\degree-\beta)}}= \frac{\sin{\gamma}}{\sin{\beta}}MBBL​=sinγsinα​, LCCN​=sin(180°−β)sinγ​=sinβsinγ​ Hence: AM‾MB‾⋅BL‾LC‾⋅CN‾NA‾=AM‾NA‾⋅BL‾MB‾⋅CN‾LC‾=sin⁡βsin⁡α⋅sin⁡αsin⁡γ⋅sin⁡γsin⁡β=1■\frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{LC}} \cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{NA}}= \frac{\overline{AM}}{\overline{NA}}\cdot \frac{\overline{BL}}{\overline{MB}}\cdot \frac{\overline{CN}}{\overline{LC}}= \frac{\sin{\beta}}{\sin{\alpha}}\cdot \frac{\sin{\alpha}}{\sin{\gamma}} \cdot \frac{\sin{\gamma}}{\sin{\beta}}=1 \tag*{$\blacksquare$}MBAM​⋅LCBL​⋅NACN​=NAAM​⋅MBBL​⋅LCCN​=sinαsinβ​⋅sinγsinα​⋅sinβsinγ​=1■

Law of Cosines For a random △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC, aaa, bbb, and ccc are the length of the sides of the triangle, and respectively, ∠A\angle A∠A, ∠B\angle B∠B, and ∠C\angle C∠C are the opposite angles, then the Law of Cosines points out: c2=a2+b2−2abcos⁡∠C c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos{\angle C} c2=a2+b2−2abcos∠C And similarly: a2=b2+c2−2bccos⁡∠A a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos{\angle A}a2=b2+c2−2bccos∠Ab2=a2+c2−2accos⁡∠B b^2=a^2+c^2-2ac\cos{\angle B}b2=a2+c2−2accos∠B Derivation Draw a prependicular line from point C to side ABABAB and intersect with it at point D, and we have: c=BD‾+AD‾=acos⁡∠B+bcos⁡∠A c=\overline{BD}+\overline{AD}=a\cos{\angle B}+b\cos{\angle A}c=BD+AD=acos∠B+bcos∠A Times ccc on both side: c2=accos⁡∠B+bccos⁡∠A c^2=ac\cos{\angle B}+bc\cos{\angle A}c2=accos∠B+bccos∠A By the same reasoning: a2=abcos⁡∠C+accos⁡∠B a^2=ab\cos{\angle C}+ac\cos{\angle B}a2=abcos∠C+accos∠Bb2=abcos⁡∠C+bccos⁡∠A b^2=ab\cos{\angle C}+bc\cos{\angle A}b2=abcos∠C+bccos∠A ∴accos⁡∠B=a2−abcos⁡∠C, bccos⁡∠A=b2−abcos⁡∠C\therefore ac\cos{\angle B}= a^2-ab\cos{\angle C}, \ bc\cos{\angle A}= b^2-ab\cos{\angle C}∴accos∠B=a2−abcos∠C, bccos∠A=b2−abcos∠C∴c2=accos⁡∠B+bccos⁡∠A=(a2−abcos⁡∠C)+(b2−abcos⁡∠C)=a2+b2−2abcos⁡∠C\therefore c^2=ac\cos{\angle B}+bc\cos{\angle A}= (a^2-ab\cos{\angle C})+(b^2-ab\cos{\angle C})=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos{\angle C} ∴c2=accos∠B+bccos∠A=(a2−abcos∠C)+(b2−abcos∠C)=a2+b2−2abcos∠C By draw the perpendicular line from different direction, similarly: a2=b2+c2−2bccos⁡∠A a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos{\angle A}a2=b2+c2−2bccos∠Ab2=a2+c2−2accos⁡∠B■ b^2=a^2+c^2-2ac\cos{\angle B} \tag*{$\blacksquare$}b2=a2+c2−2accos∠B■

Law of Sines For a random △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC, aaa, bbb, and ccc are the length of the sides of the triangle, and seperately, ∠A\angle A∠A, ∠B\angle B∠B, and ∠C\angle C∠C are the opposite angles, then the Law of Sines points out: asin⁡∠A=bsin⁡∠B=csin⁡∠C=2R \frac{a}{\sin{\angle A}}=\frac{b}{\sin{\angle B}}=\frac{c}{\sin{\angle C}}= 2Rsin∠Aa​=sin∠Bb​=sin∠Cc​=2R where RRR is the length of the radius of the circumscribed circle of △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC. Derivation ⊙O\odot O⊙O is the circumscribed circle of △ABC\triangle ABC△ABC; connect OBOBOB and OCOCOC; draw a prependicular line from OOO to side BCBCBC and intersect with it at point DDD. Note that ∠A\angle A∠A is an inscribed angle, while ∠BOC\angle BOC∠BOC is the central angle subtending BC⌢\overset{\frown}{BC}BC⌢, which means: ∠A=12∠BOC\angle A=\frac{1}{2}\angle BOC∠A=21​∠BOC∵OB=OC=R, OD⊥BC∴∠BOD=∠COD=12∠BOC=∠A∴sin⁡∠A=sin⁡∠COD=CDOC=12aR ⟹ asin⁡∠A=2R \begin{align*} &\because OB=OC=R, \ OD\perp BC \\ &\therefore \angle BOD=\angle COD= \frac{1}{2}\angle BOC=\angle A\\ &\therefore \sin{\angle A}=\sin{\angle COD}=\frac{CD}{OC}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}a}{R}\\ &\implies \frac{a}{\sin {\angle A}}= 2R \end{align*}​∵OB=OC=R, OD⊥BC∴∠BOD=∠COD=21​∠BOC=∠A∴sin∠A=sin∠COD=OCCD​=R21​a​⟹sin∠Aa​=2R​ Similarly, from other directions, we may also get: bsin⁡∠B=2R, csin⁡∠C=2R\frac{b}{\sin {\angle B}}= 2R,\ \frac{c}{\sin {\angle C}}= 2Rsin∠Bb​=2R, sin∠Cc​=2R Hence: asin⁡∠A=bsin⁡∠B=csin⁡∠C=2R■ \frac{a}{\sin{\angle A}}=\frac{b}{\sin{\angle B}}=\frac{c}{\sin{\angle C}}= 2R \tag*{$\blacksquare$}sin∠Aa​=sin∠Bb​=sin∠Cc​=2R■

sin⁡(α+β)=sin⁡αcos⁡β+cos⁡αsin⁡β\sin{\left(\alpha+\beta\right)}=\sin{\alpha}\cos{\beta}+\cos{\alpha}\sin{\beta}sin(α+β)=sinαcosβ+cosαsinβ Derivation sin⁡β=BCAB=BC1 ⟹ BC=sin⁡βcos⁡β=ACAB=AC1 ⟹ AC=cos⁡βsin⁡α=CDAC=CDcos⁡β ⟹ CD=sin⁡αcos⁡βcos⁡α=CFBC=CFsin⁡β ⟹ CF=cos⁡αsin⁡β \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{CD}{AC}=\frac{CD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies CD&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{CF}{BC}=\frac{CF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies CF&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinβ=ABBC​=1BC​⟹BCcosβ=ABAC​=1AC​⟹ACsinα=ACCD​=cosβCD​⟹CDcosα=BCCF​=sinβCF​⟹CF​=sinβ=cosβ=sinαcosβ=cosαsinβ​sin⁡(α+β)=BEAB=BE1=DF=CD+CF=sin⁡αcos⁡β+cos⁡αsin⁡β \begin{align*} \sin{(\green{\alpha}+\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{BE}{AB}=\frac{BE}{1}=DF\\ &=CD+CF\\ &=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}+\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}sin(α+β)​=ABBE​=1BE​=DF=CD+CF=sinαcosβ+cosαsinβ​■​

sin⁡(α−β)=sin⁡αcos⁡β−cos⁡αsin⁡β\sin{\left(\alpha-\beta\right)}=\sin{\alpha}\cos{\beta}-\cos{\alpha}\sin{\beta}sin(α−β)=sinαcosβ−cosαsinβ Derivation sin⁡β=BCAB=BC1 ⟹ BC=sin⁡βcos⁡β=ACAB=AC1 ⟹ AC=cos⁡βsin⁡α=ADAC=ADcos⁡β ⟹ AD=sin⁡αcos⁡βcos⁡α=BFBC=BFsin⁡β ⟹ BF=cos⁡αsin⁡β \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{AD}{AC}=\frac{AD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies AD&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{BF}{BC}=\frac{BF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies BF&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinβ=ABBC​=1BC​⟹BCcosβ=ABAC​=1AC​⟹ACsinα=ACAD​=cosβAD​⟹ADcosα=BCBF​=sinβBF​⟹BF​=sinβ=cosβ=sinαcosβ=cosαsinβ​sin⁡(α−β)=BGAB=BG1=AE=AD−ED=AD−BF=sin⁡αcos⁡β−cos⁡αsin⁡β \begin{align*} \sin{(\green{\alpha}-\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{BG}{AB}=\frac{BG}{1}=AE\\ &=AD-ED\\ &=AD-BF\\ &=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}-\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}sin(α−β)​=ABBG​=1BG​=AE=AD−ED=AD−BF=sinαcosβ−cosαsinβ​■​

cos⁡(α+β)=cos⁡αcos⁡β−sin⁡αsin⁡β\cos{\left(\alpha+\beta\right)}=\cos{\alpha}\cos{\beta}-\sin{\alpha}\sin{\beta}cos(α+β)=cosαcosβ−sinαsinβ Derivation sin⁡β=BCAB=BC1 ⟹ BC=sin⁡βcos⁡β=ACAB=AC1 ⟹ AC=cos⁡βsin⁡α=BFAC=BFsin⁡β ⟹ BF=sin⁡αsin⁡βcos⁡α=ADBC=ADcos⁡β ⟹ AD=cos⁡αcos⁡β \begin{align*} \blue{\sin{\beta}}=\frac{BC}{AB}=\frac{BC}{1}\implies BC&=\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \blue{\cos{\beta}}=\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{AC}{1}\implies AC&=\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \green{\sin{\alpha}}=\frac{BF}{AC}=\frac{BF}{\blue{\sin{\beta}}}\implies BF&=\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}}\\ \green{\cos{\alpha}}=\frac{AD}{BC}=\frac{AD}{\blue{\cos{\beta}}}\implies AD&=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}\\ \end{align*}sinβ=ABBC​=1BC​⟹BCcosβ=ABAC​=1AC​⟹ACsinα=ACBF​=sinβBF​⟹BFcosα=BCAD​=cosβAD​⟹AD​=sinβ=cosβ=sinαsinβ=cosαcosβ​cos⁡(α+β)=AEAB=AE1=AD−ED=AD−BF=cos⁡αcos⁡β−sin⁡αsin⁡β \begin{align*} \cos{(\green{\alpha}+\blue{\beta})}&=\frac{AE}{AB}=\frac{AE}{1}\\ &=AD-ED\\ &=AD-BF\\ &=\green{\cos{\alpha}}\blue{\cos{\beta}}-\green{\sin{\alpha}}\blue{\sin{\beta}} \tag*{$\blacksquare$} \end{align*}cos(α+β)​=ABAE​=1AE​=AD−ED=AD−BF=cosαcosβ−sinαsinβ​■​

Let's discover Docusaurus in less than 5 minutes.
Get started by creating a new site. Or try Docusaurus immediately with docusaurus.new.
Node.js version 20.0 or above: When installing Node.js, you are recommended to check all checkboxes related to dependencies.

Generate a new Docusaurus site using the classic template. The classic template will automatically be added to your project after you run the command: npm init docusaurus@latest my-website classic You can type this command into Command Prompt, Powershell, Terminal, or any other integrated terminal of your code editor. The command also installs all necessary dependencies you need to run Docusaurus.

Run the development server: cd my-website npm run start The cd command changes the directory you're working with. In order to work with your newly created Docusaurus site, you'll need to navigate the terminal there. The npm run start command builds your website locally and serves it through a development server, ready for you to view at http://localhost:3000/. Open docs/intro.md (this page) and edit some lines: the site reloads automatically and displays your changes.
